title: Gerunds, Infinitives & Verb Patterns — Band 7+ Reference
subtitle: Which verbs take -ing, which take to-V, and the meaning shifts that confuse Band 6 candidates
intro: After most verbs in English, you have to choose: -ing (gerund) or to + V (infinitive). 'I enjoy reading' / 'I want to read'. Get it wrong and the sentence is grammatically broken. Some verbs even change meaning depending on the choice ('I stopped smoking' ≠ 'I stopped to smoke'). This reference is the comprehensive list — memorise the patterns, eliminate the errors.

sections.title: 1. The Three Forms — Gerund / Infinitive / Bare Infinitive
sections.summary: Three verb forms can follow another verb. Each has specific contexts.

examples.plural: Reading is my hobby.
I enjoy reading.
I am good at reading. / She insisted on coming.
I appreciate your helping me.
I want to leave.
I came here to study.
I am happy to help. / It is important to study.
I don't know what to do / where to go / how to start.
You should leave. / She can swim.
She made me cry. / Let me help you. / I had him fix it.
I saw him leave. / I heard her sing.
Why not try it? / You'd better leave.

sections.title: 2. Verbs Followed by GERUND ONLY
sections.summary: Memorise these. There are about 30 essential ones — using 'to + V' after them is grammatically wrong.

items: imagine (Imagine living in a remote village)
miss (I miss living near the sea)
mind (Do you mind opening the window?)
give up (He gave up smoking last year)
tolerate (I won't tolerate being treated like that)
fancy (BrE: Do you fancy going out tonight?)
feel like (I don't feel like cooking tonight)

examples.plural: ✔ I enjoy reading.
✔ She avoided meeting him.

examples.singular: ❌ He suggested to go to the cinema.
examples.plural: ✔ He suggested going to the cinema.
✔ I gave up smoking.
He admitted NOT KNOWING the answer. (NOT 'to not know')
She regrets NOT GOING to university.

sections.title: 3. Verbs Followed by TO-INFINITIVE ONLY
sections.summary: These verbs require 'to + V'. Using -ing is wrong.

items: hope (We hope to see you soon)
afford (I can't afford to lose this job)
seem / appear (She seems to know him)
claim (He claimed to be a doctor)
wish (I wish to apply for the position)
arrange (We arranged to meet at noon)
demand (They demanded to see the manager)

examples.plural: ✔ I want to go home.
✔ She decided to study medicine.
✔ We agreed to meet at 6.

sections.title: 4. Verbs Taking BOTH — Same Meaning
sections.summary: These verbs accept either gerund or to-infinitive with no difference in meaning.

rules.pattern: Both forms, same meaning — pick whichever
items: begin / start (I began studying / I began to study)
propose (formal: He proposed leaving / to leave)
can't bear / can't stand (I can't bear waiting / to wait)

examples.plural: I love DANCING. (general activity)

examples.singular: to-V emphasises specific occasion or preference
examples.plural: I would love TO DANCE with you. (specific)

rules.note: Both correct. -ing is slightly more general; to-infinitive often has a specific or polite tone.

sections.title: 5. Verbs Taking BOTH — DIFFERENT Meaning (Critical)
sections.summary: These verbs take both forms — but the meaning changes completely. Confusing them is a Band 6 vocabulary/grammar error that examiners catch.

examples.plural: I stopped smoking last year. (= I quit)

examples.singular: stop + to V = pause in order to do
examples.plural: I stopped to smoke. (= I paused my activity in order to have a cigarette)
I remember LOCKING the door. (= I locked it; I remember doing so)

examples.singular: remember + to V = remember to do (future obligation)
examples.plural: Remember TO LOCK the door. (= don't forget to do it)

examples.singular: forget + -ing = fail to recall a past action
examples.plural: I'll never forget MEETING her. (= I met her; the memory is permanent)

examples.singular: forget + to V = fail to do something
examples.plural: I forgot TO LOCK the door. (= I didn't lock it)

examples.singular: regret + -ing = feel sorry about a past action
examples.plural: I regret SAYING that. (= I said it; now I'm sorry)

examples.singular: regret + to V = sorry to inform (formal)
examples.plural: I regret TO INFORM you that your application was unsuccessful.

examples.singular: try + -ing = experiment, do as a test
examples.plural: Try ADDING more salt. (= experiment with adding salt)

examples.singular: try + to V = attempt, make an effort
examples.plural: I tried TO OPEN the door. (= I made an effort but maybe didn't succeed)
Getting promoted means MOVING to a new city. (= involves)
I meant TO CALL you yesterday. (= I intended to)

examples.singular: go on + -ing = continue the same action
examples.plural: She went on TALKING for hours. (= continued talking)

examples.singular: go on + to V = move to next action
examples.plural: She finished her degree, then went on TO STUDY law. (= moved to next thing)

examples.singular: need + -ing = passive meaning (need to be done)
examples.plural: The car needs WASHING. (= needs to be washed)
I need TO WASH the car.

sections.tip: Memorise these 8 'meaning-shift' verbs as a list and drill 5 sentences with each (4 correct + 1 with intentional mix-up to recognise the error). They appear constantly in IELTS Reading.

sections.title: 6. After PREPOSITIONS — Always GERUND (Never to-V)
sections.summary: After ANY preposition (in, on, at, of, for, with, about, by, from, etc.), the verb MUST be -ing form.

items: good at (She is good at SOLVING problems)
interested in (I am interested in LEARNING German)
tired of (We are tired of WAITING)
afraid of (Don't be afraid of MAKING mistakes)
responsible for (Who is responsible for ORGANISING this?)
capable of (She is capable of HANDLING the project)
fond of (He is fond of TRAVELLING)
keen on (BrE: I'm keen on STARTING soon)
used to / accustomed to (I'm used to GETTING up early)
look forward to (I look forward to MEETING you)
succeed in (He succeeded in PASSING the exam)
object to (They objected to BEING treated unfairly)
apologise for (I apologise for BEING late)
thank for (Thank you for HELPING me)
stop from (Nothing can stop me from TRYING)
depend on (Success depends on WORKING hard)
give up on (I won't give up on SOLVING this)
warn against (She warned us against INVESTING there)

rules.pattern: Common error — 'to' as preposition vs 'to' as infinitive marker

examples.singular: ❌ I look forward to MEET you. (treated 'to' as infinitive marker)
examples.plural: ✔ I look forward to MEETING you. (here 'to' is a preposition!)
✔ I'm used to WORKING hard. ('used to + -ing' = accustomed to)

rules.note: When 'to' is a PREPOSITION (look forward to / used to / accustomed to / object to / opposed to / committed to / dedicated to), it takes -ING. When 'to' is an INFINITIVE marker (want to / decide to / try to), it takes V. Test: replace 'to' with 'to + a noun'. If it sounds natural ('I look forward to the meeting'), it's a preposition → use -ING.

examples.plural: The importance of GETTING enough sleep cannot be overstated.
The possibility of REACHING an agreement seems remote.

sections.title: 7. Verb + OBJECT + Infinitive (or -ing)
sections.summary: Many verbs take an object before the infinitive. A small group take an object before -ing. Get the pattern right.

items: ask sb to do (I asked her to leave)
tell sb to do (He told me to wait)
want sb to do (I want you to come)
expect sb to do (We expect them to arrive)
advise sb to do (She advised me to study)
allow sb to do (They allowed us to enter)
encourage sb to do (His teacher encouraged him to apply)
force sb to do (She was forced to leave)
invite sb to do (He invited me to dinner)
persuade sb to do (I persuaded her to stay)
remind sb to do (Remind me to call)
warn sb to do / not to do (She warned us to be careful)
teach sb to do (My father taught me to drive)
help sb (to) do (Can you help me (to) move?)
make sb do (She made me cry)
have sb do (causative: I'll have him fix it)
see sb do (I saw him leave — completed action)
hear sb do (We heard her sing — completed action)
feel sb do (I felt her hand touch mine — completed action)
watch sb do (We watched the team play)
rules.note: Sense verbs (see, hear, watch, feel) can also take -ING for action in progress: 'I saw him crossing the street' (in progress) vs 'I saw him cross the street' (complete action).

examples.plural: She made me cry.
I was made to cry.
They let her go.
She was allowed to go. ('Let' has no normal passive — use 'allow' instead)

sections.title: 8. Verb Patterns in IELTS Writing & Speaking
sections.summary: Specific high-yield patterns to deploy in IELTS contexts.

examples.plural: I tend to agree with the first view.
I am inclined to disagree with this perspective.
It is worth considering both sides of the argument.
There is no point in denying the seriousness of the issue.
Governments should consider investing more in renewable energy.
Educators must focus on developing critical thinking.
Society must be committed to addressing inequality.
I'm used to waking up early for work.
I've got used to living in a big city.
I'd rather stay home than go out tonight.
It is essential to balance work and family life.
There is a risk of losing cultural identity in a globalised world.
Young people need to be aware of protecting their data online.

sections.title: 9. Top Verb-Pattern Errors at the Band 6 → 7+ Threshold
sections.summary: Specific mistakes that pull Band 7 candidates down.

rules.pattern: 1. Wrong form after 'enjoy / avoid / suggest / consider'

examples.plural: ✔ I enjoy playing football.

examples.singular: ❌ He suggested to go to the cinema.
examples.plural: ✔ He suggested going to the cinema.

rules.pattern: 2. Wrong form after 'want / decide / hope / agree'

examples.plural: ✔ I want to go home.
✔ We agreed to meet at 6.

examples.singular: ❌ I look forward to meet you.
examples.plural: ✔ I look forward to meeting you.
✔ I'm used to working hard.

examples.singular: ❌ I am interested in to learn German.
examples.plural: ✔ I am interested in learning German.
✔ Thank you for helping me.

examples.singular: ❌ I stopped to smoke last year. (means: I paused to have a cigarette)
examples.plural: ✔ I stopped smoking last year. (= I quit)

examples.singular: ❌ Remember locking the door before you leave.
examples.plural: ✔ Remember to lock the door before you leave. (= future obligation)
✔ She made me cry.
✔ Let me help you.

rules.pattern: 8. Wrong negative form ('to not' vs 'not to')

examples.plural: ✔ I decided not to go. ('not' before 'to')
✔ She admitted not knowing. (negative -ing)
✔ I am happy to meet you. (adjectives take to-V for reaction)
(busy + -ing for ongoing activity)

sections.tip: Build a personal verb-pattern flashcard deck: front = verb, back = pattern + example. Drill 10/day. After 14 days, the top 50 patterns become automatic. This is the single most effective drill for eliminating gerund/infinitive errors.

checklist: I always use -ING after enjoy / avoid / suggest / consider / recommend / deny / admit / mind.
I always use TO-V after want / decide / hope / expect / agree / refuse / promise / plan.
I always use -ING after ANY preposition (in, on, at, of, for, with, by, from, about).
I always use -ING after 'look forward to / used to / accustomed to / committed to' (here 'to' is a preposition, not infinitive marker).
I distinguish 'stop + -ing' (quit) from 'stop + to V' (pause in order to).
I distinguish 'remember + -ing' (past memory) from 'remember + to V' (future obligation).
I never put 'to' after make / let / have (causative): 'She made me cry', not 'made me to cry'.
I form negatives correctly: 'decided NOT TO V' (not 'to not V') and 'admitted NOT V-ing' (not 'to not -ing').
I use bare infinitive after modals (can swim, should leave) — never 'to'.
I deploy at least 3 different verb-pattern collocations per Band 7+ Task 2 essay (consider doing, focus on doing, committed to doing).
